data.title: US Senate Republicans pass measure to move forward on Trump's tax cuts
data.description:
Key points:
  • Plunging stock market hovers over fiscal outlook
  • House Republicans now must weigh Senate's work
  • Democrats warn that Medicaid under threat

The U.S. Senate approved a Republican budget blueprint early on Saturday that aims to extend trillions of dollars worth of President Donald Trump's 2017 tax cuts and sharply reduce government spending.

The 51-48 vote, following a late-night legislative session, unlocks a maneuver called budget reconciliation that will allow Republicans to bypass the Senate's filibuster - a rule that imposes a 60-vote threshold on most legislation - and pass Trump's tax, border security and military priorities later this year without Democratic votes.

"Tonight, the Senate took one small step toward reconciliation and one giant leap toward making the tax cuts permanent, securing the border, providing much-needed help for the military and finally cutting wasteful Washington spending," Senate Budget Committee Chairman Lindsey Graham said.

Two Republicans - Senators Susan Collins and Rand Paul - joined Democrats in opposing the measure.

The Senate's action sent the measure on to the Republican-led House of Representatives, which is expected to take it up next week.

Non-partisan analysts say the Trump agenda, if enacted, would add about $5.7 trillion to the federal government's debt over the next decade. Senate Republicans contend the cost is $1.5 trillion, saying that the effects of extending existing tax policy that was scheduled to expire at the end of this year should not be counted in the measure's cost.

The measure also aims to raise the federal government's debt ceiling by $5 trillion, a move Congress has to make by summer or risk defaulting on $36.6 trillion in debt. It aims to partly offset the deficit-raising costs of tax cuts by cutting spending. Democrats have warned that Republican targets would imperil the Medicaid health insurance program for low-income Americans.

Republicans warned that allowing the 2017 tax cuts to expire would hit Americans hard, imposing a 22% tax hike on the average taxpayer. The cuts, Trump's signature legislative achievement of his first term, reduced the top corporate tax rate to 21% from 35%, a move that is not set to expire.

The remainder of the cuts, for individual Americans, were set to expire, a decision made to limit the 2017 bill's deficit-raising effects.

"Donald Trump has betrayed the American people. Tonight, Senate Republicans joined him in that betrayal. In voting for this bill, Senate Republicans sided with billionaires against the middle class, in total obeisance to Donald Trump," Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer of New York said after the vote.

BRUTAL SELL-OFF

Hanging over the debate, which began late on Thursday, was a brutal stock market sell-off following Trump's sweeping new trade tariffs, which economists warned will drive up prices and could trigger a recession.

Some Republicans said economic uncertainty could slow the path forward for Trump's agenda if market weakness continues.

"My concern is, if we are having the kind of conversation today three weeks from now, then the distraction will be so great that it will slow down what we try to do," Republican Senator Thom Tillis told reporters.

During a six-hour "vote-a-rama" session to consider amendments, Senate Republicans altered the blueprint to add a deficit-neutral reserve fund to help protect Medicaid and the Medicare healthcare program for the elderly.

Republicans also turned away dozens of Democratic amendments aimed at rescinding Trump trade tariffs and protecting Medicaid, Medicare, nutrition support for low-income women and children, the Social Security retirement system, veterans benefits and other government assistance.

Republican Senators Lisa Murkowski, Josh Hawley and Collins backed Democratic measures to safeguard social safety-net programs, but their support was not enough.

If House Republicans get their way, Congress could enact $2 trillion in spending cuts by overhauling Medicaid and food assistance programs and by eliminating popular environmental policies.

The budget blueprint would also make room for tighter security measures along the U.S. border with Mexico, fund administration efforts to significantly ramp up immigrant deportations and bolster U.S. military readiness.

data.title: Global polymer market synopsis
data.description: AsiaHDPE: This week, HDPE grade prices remained constant in the Asian region. Global oil prices experienced a decline as markets weighed the potential impact of a dampened demand outlook stemming from U.S. tariff policies, implying a shift in focus from the effect of imposition of sanctions on oil-producing nations. In China, the import prices of polyethylene (PE) are under persistent pressure due to weak purchasing interest and falling domestic market prices. The decline in import price netbacks has led overseas suppliers to redirect their shipments to markets with tighter supply conditions. While rising maintenance costs may provide some short-term price support, downstream buyers are cautiously replenishing their inventories in preparation for the holiday season. However, improvements in supply and demand are limited. U.S. dollar prices saw a slight increase from the previous week, bolstered by moderate trading activity and a small appreciation of the RMB. The PE market in Southeast Asia is anticipated to experience a slowdown in early April, primarily due to holiday-related disruptions such as Eid ul-Fitr in Indonesia and Malaysia, as well as Songkran in Thailand. There has been a decline in purchasing sentiment, as many converters have already acquired adequate supplies and are hesitant to increase their inventories. In Vietnam, market activity has diminished; however, there is some price support from a major supplier in the Middle East, although transactions remain unconfirmed and limited in scope. The lower segment of the price spectrum continues to be pressured by an oversupply, which includes competitively priced HDPE from the U.S. being resold by traders and end-users. Looking forward, demand is likely to stay subdued in the first half of April, with the possibility of a rebound later in the month, contingent upon supply conditions and overall economic factorsIn India, current PE market conditions hint at stable to slightly firm pricing trends in the short term. In summary, although supply limitations may sustain higher prices, the sluggish demand is anticipated to mitigate any considerable price increases. This decline occurred despite limited regional supplies, primarily due to weak demand from downstream markets and an increase in the availability of PP raffia from China. In contrast, producers in the Middle East have kept their PP prices steady compared to the previous month, attributed to supply constraints during the maintenance period, which has helped maintain stable prices for dutiable PP raffia in the region. This behavior indicates a strategic approach where buyers assess the risks associated with potential price changes and supply chain factors in relation to their existing inventory and production timelines. This cautious attitude among buyers points to a market that reacts to immediate needs rather than committing to long-term polystyrene purchases. Consequently, suppliers may need to adjust their strategies, emphasizing flexibility and responsiveness to evolving market conditions to better align with buyer behavior. ABS: ABS prices were assessed lower in the Asian region this week. Global oil prices experienced a decline as markets weighed the potential impact of a dampened demand outlook stemming from U.S. tariff policies, implying a shift in focus from the effect of imposition of sanctions on oil-producing nations. The Asian ABS CFR China marker experienced a decline, dropping below the offered prices due to a sluggish domestic market and decreasing upstream feedstock costs. ABS producers reduced their offers from the prior week as purchasing interest continued to be low. The producer anticipates that demand will remain lacklustre in the first half of the year, with no evident interest in purchasing. Domestic demand remains stagnant, while rigid demand has reached a state of stability. Overall, foreign trade exports are experiencing minimal growth, contributing to the ongoing weakness in supply and demand fundamentals. Current macroeconomic expectations and costs are reinforcing this short-term weakness. There are also concerns regarding the effects of increased tariffs on the export of both raw materials and finished products. Furthermore, following a decline in prices in April within key foreign market demand areas, China's exports have faced pressure this week, making it challenging for demand to gain momentum. The market is currently characterized by a relatively abundant supply, with demand entering a wait-and-see phase. Additionally, the reduction in production loads or planned maintenance at factories in certain regions has yet to be implemented. While there is currently little evidence of increased freight costs from China, some market participants anticipate that this may become a significant factor in the coming months as Southeast Asian buyers look to replenish their stocks following various public holidays in the region. The ongoing Eid holiday and the Thai New Year celebrations are currently shaping the balance of the Southeast Asian s-PVC market, further diminishing import buying interest as customers prefer to source from local suppliers to manage inventory levels. Additionally, demand for PVC from customers has been slow to materialize following a recent fire at an EDC unit in Indonesia, which led to reduced PVC operating rates at the integrated facility. Most customers have expressed minimal concern regarding short-term supply security. As with other grades, the impact on HDPE supply remains uncertain until there is greater clarity regarding tariffs and the disruptions caused by their announcement. This week, stakeholders in the European polyethylene (PE) market adopted a cautious stance amid an unclear supply forecast for April and the second quarter. The potential imposition of tariffs by the EU on HDPE imports from the United States is central to the current dynamics. Ongoing consultations have led to increasing expectations that tariffs may be enacted as early as next week, with no indications of easing trade tensions thus far. Furthermore, the EU is reportedly considering expanding its proposed tariffs to include the US services sector in response to the 25% tariffs recently announced on all automotive imports into the US, indicating a possible escalation of these tensions. This situation may prompt some sellers to seek price increases, especially if tariffs are implemented. However, it is anticipated that global trade flows will adjust after the second quarter, with Europe likely to receive more HDPE imports from regions such as the Middle East and Asia-Pacific. The shipping times for these imports may be longer than those from the US, potentially granting European producers greater pricing power for an extended duration, particularly in times of supply vulnerability. Many buyers have hesitated to make firm commitments thus far, despite facing challenging circumstances. The degree of commitment to purchases varies across the value chain, with those involved in film grades experiencing a more pressing need to replenish their inventories. Conversely, the infrastructure, construction, and rigid and durable goods sectors have encountered weaker conditions, allowing HDPE buyers in those areas to remain relaxed, even declining some spot offers for the time being. Buyers currently perceive adequate availability and are cautious about the potential decline in HDPE prices if the anticipated tariffs do not materialize. Nonetheless, many are in need of restocking, having postponed part of their procurement in March due to expectations of falling HDPE prices in April prior to the announcement of the proposed tariffs. Overall demand remains subdued across various value chains, although there has been some relative improvement in March. Many market participants anticipate that weak demand conditions will continue amid a stagnant economic outlook, with the ongoing trade conflict with the US suggesting the possibility of cost-push inflation, which could negatively impact consumption. European LLDPE spot price has decreased due to a lack of robust market activity, while negotiations for April contracts remain uncertain. The supply of LLDPE in Europe is significantly reliant on imports, mainly from the United States. However, in the absence of clear information, buyers are expected to resist any price hikes. Ongoing discussions suggest that these tariffs may be implemented as early as April 13, with no indications of a reduction in trade tensions. Furthermore, the EU is contemplating broadening its proposed tariffs to encompass the US services sector in retaliation for the 25% tariffs imposed by the US on all automotive imports, indicating a possible escalation in trade conflicts in the near future. PP: This week, PP prices eased in the European region. This week, the polypropylene (PP) market in Europe is experiencing a downturn, influenced by a combination of abundant import offers and subdued demand, along with growing concerns regarding the effects of new US automotive tariffs. Prices in Europe have declined, driven by lower offers from international suppliers. It is expected that European production will continue at reduced operating rates, reflecting the lacklustre demand from converters. Overall, supply and demand fundamentals remained largely stable during the week, with market discussions for the upcoming month centering on the potential for further price reductions following a decrease in contract pricing for the essential feedstock, propylene. Domestic supply is widely available throughout most of the market, which has intensified the demand weakness from critical sectors such as automotive and construction. Notably, demand from the automotive sector has been particularly weak, following adjustments at various vehicle production facilities across the continent over the past six months. During the first half of the week, market activity was relatively quiet as participants awaited the resolution of contract pricing for the key PP feedstock, propylene. The anticipated decrease in pricing aligned with expectations from value chain stakeholders, who had predicted a decline due to falling spot prices for feedstock naphtha in March, compared to the February average. This decline has set the tone for PP pricing discussions for the upcoming month, with market participants generally reporting lower spot prices as they approach April. The import market has experienced an increase in the availability of competitively priced products from outside Europe, capturing the interest of converters during their procurement activities. European polypropylene (PP) producers are also contending with the challenge posed by imports from the Middle East and Asia-Pacific, exacerbated by declining freight rates. Although lead times for these imported goods remain lengthy, a structurally weakened demand within Europe has compelled some domestic producers to actively seek avenues to offload their existing inventory, thereby exerting downward pressure on PP spot prices. This week, prices have decreased due to a subdued market, and further declines in PP spot prices are anticipated in the upcoming weeks. Such trends may narrow the import arbitrage, diminishing the attractiveness of imports in a European market characterized by weak demand prospects. However, the extent of any reductions in European spot prices will influence the market's capacity to resist imports. Additionally, markets in the Asia-Pacific region are currently saturated, prompting sellers in that area to continue exploring export arbitrage opportunities where possible. The pricing forecast indicates a bearish trend for April, driven by lower feedstock prices and a gradual increase in supply length, primarily due to diminished demand in March as buyers postponed some of their procurement. This situation has allowed buyers to gain leverage in a backwardated market, leading many to adopt a cautious approach with the intention of replenishing PP volumes at reduced prices in April. Furthermore, the temporary closures of many converters' plants during the Easter holiday may impact their overall inventory replenishment needs, raising concerns for PP sellers. Numerous market participants anticipate that weak demand conditions will persist throughout the value chains amid a stagnant economic outlook, with the ongoing trade conflict with the US suggesting potential cost-push inflation on the horizon, which could negatively affect consumption. PS: This week, PS prices trended lower in the European region. The European polystyrene market experienced a lacklustre week, despite announcements from producers regarding price reductions. Regional manufacturers reported a decrease of ¬45-50 per metric ton for both general purpose and high-impact polystyrene. This adjustment followed a ¬58 per metric ton decline in the styrene contract price over the month. Demand remained weak in anticipation of the styrene settlement, as market participants awaited clarity on feedstock price fluctuations before making further market moves. A source indicated that producers were inquiring about purchases two months in advance and were producing quantities based on these orders. Although the market was adequately supplied during the week, the source noted that if producers continued to focus solely on fulfilling advance orders, it could affect the availability of spot supplies in the upcoming months. A decrease in domestic spot availability might drive an increase in demand for imported materials; however, reports suggest that imports are currently facing delays in reaching Europe. Several factors have led to the decline in polystyrene prices, with one of the main contributors being the reduced demand from key downstream industries, such as electric vehicle manufacturing and construction. These sectors, which are significant consumers of polystyrene, have experienced a downturn in activity, resulting in lower consumption rates. Additionally, a contraction in the manufacturing sector, prompted by a decrease in new orders and production output, has further aggravated the situation. The polystyrene market is currently facing an oversupply situation. Elevated inventory levels, coupled with steady production from leading manufacturers, have contributed to a strong supply chain. However, the arrival of competitively priced imports from regions such as Asia has intensified competition for domestic producers, compelling them to reduce their prices. Although European manufacturers have cut back on production in response to waning demand, the supply remains adequate to satisfy current consumption levels, which continues to exert downward pressure on prices. This persistent decline highlights the ongoing challenges within the polystyrene market. Additionally, the decrease in polystyrene prices can be attributed to the destocking of both raw materials and finished products. Companies have been actively working to lower their inventories, resulting in diminished purchasing activity and further weakening demand for polystyrene. Looking ahead, the outlook for the Polystyrene market is uncertain. Current conditions indicate that price pressures are likely to persist, although potential stabilization in styrene prices and increased demand from industries such as packaging and electronics may provide some relief. Ultimately, the market's trajectory will largely depend on broader economic trends and the manufacturing sector's ability to recover from its present downturn. The existing price trend reflects these ongoing difficulties, with future market developments expected to be shaped by wider economic conditions and possible enhancements in supply chain logistics. ABS: This week, ABS spot prices gained in the European region. This week, spot prices for acrylonitrile-butadiene-styrene (ABS) in the European market experienced an increase due to restricted product availability, with spot prices rising while contract prices remained stable. Market participants expressed cautious optimism regarding ABS, highlighting its stronger performance compared to many polyolefin markets, attributed to its wider range of applications. Demand from the electrical and electronics sectors was reported to be robust. However, there was a more pessimistic outlook for the automotive industry, despite the possibility that a surge in electric vehicle production could enhance ABS demand; a distributor indicated that such an increase is unlikely within the next two years. In light of the European Commission's antidumping investigation into materials from South Korea and UNV1, a distributor observed that offers from these regions closely aligned with European prices. The source also mentioned that concerns over potential backdated duties have significantly reduced import volumes, although China may be able to fill the gap. Spot activity remained limited, and there was still stock available. PVC: This week, PVC prices rolled over in the European region. The European polyvinyl chloride spot market exhibited stability during the week as participants awaited the effects of the ethylene MCP settlement on PVC pricing. Attention shifted towards the European ethylene industry's settled contract price for April, which showed a decrease compared to March. Average naphtha spot prices for March also declined from the previous month's figures. In the PVC contract market, most stakeholders adopted a cautious approach, monitoring how the drop in ethylene prices would influence PVC costs. A few producers were reported to be negotiating at a rollover from March, citing difficulties in maintaining producer margins. While some sources predicted a reduction from March, they acknowledged the current uncertainty in making accurate forecasts. Demand throughout March and into early April mirrored that of January and February, with only a modest uptick in seasonal demand from the construction sector. Although buying interest from the construction industry in Spain was stronger than in other parts of Europe, one source noted a slight deceleration in orders from Spanish construction clients. Across Europe, expectations were for a slight decline in demand in April due to the Easter holidays. Interest in materials from Asia persisted, but some market participants hesitated to place orders until they had clearer insights into April's contract prices. One source suggested that if producers maintained firm pricing, it could result in an increase in imports from Asia. Imports from Mexico were reported to continue throughout March and into April, with normal availability anticipated to resume in May. Supply in Europe continues to be abundant, despite the ongoing and scheduled maintenance activities in the region and a decrease in imports. This situation is largely influenced by diminished demand from key customers and sectors. Last week, a suspension of operations at an s-PVC production facility in Poland was lifted. However, PVC production units in Belgium faced challenges in their integrated chlorine cell room, resulting in reduced production rates since last week. Although plans for a restart are in progress, the duration of this issue remains uncertain. Additionally, a PVC production unit in Germany is set for maintenance in April, lasting one month, while maintenance in the Netherlands has been extended by an additional week beyond its initial six-week schedule. This week, spot PVC prices have remained stable, with import offers primarily coming from China, Taiwan, and South Korea. However, interest in purchasing imports is limited due to the overall decline in PVC demand across the region and decreased offtake from major converters. PET: This week, PET spot prices declined in the European region. The European PET market has experienced a downward trend, driven by factors such as weak demand, decreased production costs, and falling feedstock prices. Despite this, the overall demand for PET in Europe remains below expectations, with industry players anticipating the start of the preform season to boost volumes. Consequently, softer prices were reported this week due to low demand and competitive offers from Asia. Additionally, the feedstock PTA market continued to face tight supply, with market participants indicating a heightened focus on the Asian market for feedstock due to production challenges across the continent. In the European polyethylene terephthalate spot market, there was a notable increase in interest for imported materials this week, amid ongoing constraints in feedstock supply. Market players observed a rising demand for imported materials, as consumers sought to secure additional supplies. Steady shipments of imported PET are anticipated throughout the second quarter of 2025, as buyers of virgin PET are reportedly capitalizing on lower prices and finalizing agreements for May and June. A convergence of critical issues is creating apprehension within the industry regarding its long-term sustainability. In addition to widespread challenges such as inflation and elevated energy costs, the reality is that the prices of materials�both virgin PET and rPET�are currently exceptionally high. The current condition of the EU recycling sector also plays a significant role in these discussions. Growth in this area is nearly stagnant, hindered by soaring production and energy expenses, diminished demand for recycled plastics within the EU, and the influx of inexpensive virgin and recycled plastics from abroad. The presence of more affordable foreign PET imports has emerged as a significant obstacle, with stark statistics illustrating this trend. The surge in imports has driven down regional PET prices, resulting in EU PET margins reaching unsustainable levels, which has led some producers to cease operations. Looking forward to 2025, there is a sense of cautious optimism, with many stakeholders hoping that the market will stabilize rather than decline further, potentially showing gradual recovery as the peak season approaches. One buyer remarked that while demand remains steady, it is far from strong, and he does not foresee any substantial increase in consumption for the year. Overall market demand has been lackluster, with minimal inquiries from the downstream sector. Nevertheless, supply constraints for PET have persisted during this period. Limited availability of feedstock has forced manufacturers to adjust their operations. Although the tight supply of PTA has eased, coinciding with the resumption of operations and the postponement of maintenance turnarounds, overall demand continues to be weak amid cautious procurement and limited seasonal activity. USHDPE: This week, HDPE export prices crashed in the US. US spot export prices for HDPE have remained in the stable to lower zone, with injection-grade resin reaching a four-year low this week. This decline is attributed to an oversupply, weak market fundamentals, and uncertainties surrounding tariffs, which have contributed to a sluggish trading environment. The demand for HDPE in the export market is being negatively impacted by these poor fundamentals. Participants in the market have indicated a consistent global supply of HDPE throughout March, with decreasing producer offers noted as the month advanced. Export prices are either stable or slightly reduced, varying by grade, as demand continues to be weak. Certain export markets, particularly in Europe, are constrained by tariff concerns. The pessimistic outlook for HDPE exports is largely due to an unexpected oversupply resulting from stalled tariff negotiations affecting US purchases, overproduction from a regional producer's plant in the first quarter, and recent capacity expansions in China. Spot prices are beginning to decline, with reports indicating that prices for most domestic grades have fallen since last month. One producer has been noted for offering particularly competitive prices for HDPE grades. Demand in March has been relatively stable, with producers characterizing it as healthy, while many buyers view it as lacklustre. There are growing concerns among market participants regarding demand as they approach April, with various tariff threats creating uncertainty. Although some believe that tariffs could eventually enhance demand in the US if manufacturing is brought back domestically, sources indicate that this is not a short-term solution, as establishing new production lines requires time. LDPE: This week, LDPE export prices rolled over in the US. US spot export prices for LDPE remained unchanged during the week as market participants awaited the outcomes of tariff discussions amidst subdued demand and low purchasing activity. The uncertainty surrounding the impending tariff announcements has resulted in a stagnant market, with several participants reporting a lack of activity. Demand continues to be weak, particularly as certain export markets, such as Europe, face limitations due to tariff concerns. LDPE prices have remained relatively stable, partly due to sustained demand in specific Southeast Asian markets. However, one trader indicated that the longevity of this demand is uncertain, suggesting that prices might decrease by a few cents as early as April. In March, demand has been relatively consistent, with producers characterizing it as healthy, while many buyers view it as underwhelming. Market participants have voiced apprehensions about demand as they approach April, given the various tariff threats that contribute to market uncertainty. Although there is some optimism that tariffs could enhance demand in the US if manufacturing is relocated back to the country, sources have noted that this is not a short-term solution, as setting up new production lines requires time. LLDPE: This week, LLDPE export prices down adjusted in the US. US spot export prices for LLDPE experienced a decline week over week, as market participants remained cautious while awaiting announcements regarding US tariffs. The reduction in offer levels contributed to the drop in prices. The market continues to show weakness, particularly as certain export avenues, such as those to Europe, are constrained by tariff-related concerns. In March, demand has been relatively stable, with producers characterizing it as healthy, while many buyers perceive it as underwhelming. There are growing apprehensions among market participants regarding demand as they approach April, with various tariff threats introducing a degree of uncertainty. Although some believe that the tariffs could eventually enhance demand in the US if manufacturing is brought back domestically, sources indicate that this is not a short-term solution, as setting up new production lines requires significant time and investment. PP: This week, PP export prices were left unchanged in the US. US spot export prices for polypropylene remained stable over the week, as uncertainties surrounding tariffs and competitive global markets continued to restrict trading activities. Market participants noted that there were no offers for US polypropylene during the week, as the market remains in a state of pause, awaiting clearer information on tariffs. In addition to tariff concerns, competitive pricing from Asia poses ongoing challenges for US polypropylene exports. Reports from market sources indicated minimal to no export activity, with Asian and Middle Eastern suppliers maintaining a strong presence in the global polypropylene market. Typically, resin from Asia is the most competitively priced worldwide, with US polypropylene only gaining traction when freight rates from Asia become excessively high. However, current low freight rates from Asia have provided an advantage to Asian producers. For US polypropylene exports to become competitive, market participants have emphasized the need for a reduction in polymer-grade propylene (PGP) costs. In March, contract prices for polymer-grade propylene decreased by 4 cents per pound to 44 cents per pound, yet there remains scepticism about whether this reduction will translate into lower polypropylene offers. Production levels in March have been relatively stable, with no major issues reported in the market. A Canadian producer was noted to be operating at reduced levels due to complications following the start-up of its propane dehydrogenation (PDH) unit, although this information could not be immediately verified. Sources speculated that producers might need to lower their operating rates in April if various tariff announcements are implemented, as these tariffs could significantly hinder export opportunities for US producers, even to Canada. Canadian buyers have expressed considerable concern regarding tariffs and are actively seeking alternative sourcing options. One market participant mentioned that some Canadian buyers are contemplating sourcing from Ecuador, although the freight costs from that country to Canada are prohibitively high. Market participants have voiced apprehensions regarding demand as April approaches, with various tariff threats creating a degree of uncertainty. Although there is some optimism that these tariffs might enhance demand in the US by encouraging the re-shoring of manufacturing, sources indicate that this is not a short-term solution, as establishing new production lines requires time. Reports suggest that certain Canadian converters have already relocated some of their production to the US to circumvent tariffs on imported polypropylene from the US to Canada, as well as tariffs on end-use products manufactured in Canada and exported to the US. Export activity remains quite restricted, as US prices are still perceived as too high to be competitive in the global market, compounded by the threat of tariffs that limit potential export opportunities, particularly to Mexico and Canada. PS: This week, PS export prices plunged in the US. The U.S. Polystyrene market has seen considerable declines, with prices plummeting significantly compared to the previous week. This sharp decrease is attributed to a reduction in the upstream contract price of benzene, which fell by 59 cents per gallon over the month. Industry sources indicate that U.S. prices are uncompetitive in the export market relative to Asian Polystyrene prices. The decline can be linked to diminished manufacturing activity, persistent high inflation, and moderate demand across various sectors, including automotive and consumer goods. Despite rising production costs, the surplus of polystyrene in the market has prevented any significant price increases. Manufacturers have successfully maintained their inventory levels, even in the face of logistical difficulties and decreased demand in certain areas, such as construction, where demand has remained consistently average. Nevertheless, the construction sector continues to show only average demand, which has constrained the possibility of a more substantial price rise. As a result, Polystyrene prices are expected to remain under pressure. PVC: This week, PVC export prices were fell in the US. The US polyvinyl chloride spot export market continues to experience weak demand, largely due to ongoing uncertainties surrounding tariffs. While supply has been reported as limited, market participants have indicated that product availability is not an issue. Despite the constraints in the export market, there has been no significant disruption in market fundamentals, as weak demand persists. Many have noted that demand has not improved, primarily due to the unclear trade environment. Transactions were reported at lower price points throughout the week, resulting in a decline in US PVC prices for the week ending. Offers for April were noted to have decreased slightly. A few producers were actively negotiating volumes for April, while two others encountered production challenges due to maintenance activities or upstream complications. Market insights reveal that a considerable number of participants are currently in a holding pattern, awaiting tariff announcements and responses from various countries, with minimal expectations for improvement in the near term. Given the challenging demand landscape, some participants suggested that US producers might adopt strategies akin to those of producers in other regions by curtailing their volumes. Nevertheless, it was reported that regional producers plan to complete maintenance operations by mid-April, while others may finish by the end of the month. Despite some maintenance activities in March, PVC availability remains adequate for domestic customers. While certain producers will continue maintenance into April, others are preparing to increase their operating rates in the upcoming weeks. PVC converters in the housing industry exhibited a more cautious outlook compared to those involved in public infrastructure projects. This includes sectors such as flooring, windows and doors, and interior profiles, which experienced a slowdown in inventory turnover among their downstream clients, leading to supply chain bottlenecks in March. Many participants in the housing market are wary about short-term demand forecasts, particularly as housing permits have decreased since November 2024. Actual construction activity is lagging behind the number of permits issued, raising concerns that the upcoming spring construction season may not meet the levels seen in 2024. The growing disparity in outlooks among various end users, coupled with the limited effects of production disruptions, hindered producers' attempts to achieve significant price increases in March. Although market fundamentals and historical trends suggest a potential rise in consumption at the onset of the building season, uncertainties surrounding tariffs and the overall state of the US economy have hampered both short- and medium-term planning for many market participants. US PVC stakeholders have yet to experience any direct repercussions from the ongoing discussions and implementation of tariffs on imported goods by the Trump Administration, and most US buyers do not anticipate any disruptions or price hikes related to these tariffs. However, tariffs could negatively impact consumer demand in the construction sector by increasing the costs of other building materials, or in the automotive industry by raising production expenses as parts and supply chains extend experience a stretch across North America. Traders have indicated that a rise in imports from China into US-dominated markets may exert downward pressure on prices in the upcoming weeks. Some producers noted that heightened domestic demand, along with reduced operating rates due to maintenance, gives them leverage in deciding their approach to the export market, with some hesitant to further lower export prices. Traders consider the current prices in the United States to be uncompetitive, particularly as Chinese materials are arriving on the west coast of South America and in certain regions of North Africa�markets where US pricing had previously been advantageous. It is anticipated that operating rates will increase in the upcoming weeks as maintenance activities conclude; however, the availability of export supplies and the trend of export prices will likely depend on the levels of domestic consumption. PET: This week, PET prices continued remain constant in the US. In the US market, PET prices exhibited a stable trend throughout the week. The PET spot market experienced limited purchasing activity as stakeholders awaited the announcement of tariffs on US imports. Trade uncertainties persisted in Mexico, following US President Donald Trump's earlier statement in March regarding the postponement of previously imposed tariffs on Mexico and Canada. While local US suppliers remain optimistic about PET prices, the supply in the US is insufficient. Demand for PET from downstream markets was reported to be lower this week, despite stable feedstock costs. Market participants noted that the fierce competition between recycled and virgin PET further impacted demand. On the production front, prices for essential feedstocks, MEG and PTA, remained steady, allowing downstream manufacturers and suppliers to keep their prices stable without fluctuations. In the major Southeast Asian markets, offers from Vietnam and Thailand were reported at consistent levels compared to the previous week; however, sellers expressed reluctance to accept lower bids during negotiations this week, attributing this to rising crude oil prices. In the upstream sector, ongoing discussions regarding contract prices for US Gulf paraxylene and purified terephthalic acid were reported throughout the week. Paraxylene is a key component in the production of PTA, which is then used to manufacture PET. A market participant indicated that these negotiations are expected to conclude sometime in April.

data.title: Nissan considers transferring some domestic production to US, Nikkei says
data.description:
Nissan Motor is considering shifting some domestic production of U.S.-bound vehicles to the U.S., the Nikkei reported on Saturday, as President Donald Trump ramps up trade tariffs on nations worldwide.

As early as this summer, Nissan plans to reduce production at its Fukuoka factory in western Japan and shift some manufacturing of its Rogue SUV to the United States to mitigate the impact of Trump's tariffs, the business newspaper said, without citing the source of its information.

The Japanese automaker's Rogue SUV, a key model in the U.S. market, is now produced in Fukuoka and the United States, the report said.

On Thursday, Nissan said it would not take new orders from the U.S. for two Mexican-built Infiniti SUVs after earlier Trump tariff announcements, marking, a drastic scale-back of its operations at a joint venture plant.

The automaker now plans to maintain two shifts of production of the Rogue at its Smyrna, Tennessee, plant after announcing in January it would end one of the two shifts this month.

Nissan sold about 920,000 vehicles in the U.S. last year, of which about 16% were exported from Japan, the Nikkei said, adding the planned production shift could hit local suppliers' businesses.

data.title: Ethereum Risks Drop To $1,550 If It Doesn’t Reclaim Key Resistance – What’s Next For ETH?
data.description:
Ethereum (ETH) has seen a 17% drop in the last month, trading below $1,850 for the past few days. Amid its current performance, an analyst has warned investors the cryptocurrency risks dropping to 17-month lows if it fails to reclaim key resistance levels.

Ethereum Could See Drop To $1,550

Ethereum has been trading below a key support zone for the past two days, hovering between $1,750-$1,840 after failing to recover the $1,900 mark on Wednesday. The second-largest cryptocurrency by market capitalization lost its 15-month range in early March, dropping below $2,100 for the first time since December 2023.

Since losing this level, ETH has seen its worst performance in seven years, recording a negative monthly close for the fourth consecutive month. Analyst Rekt Capital highlighted that this performance validated Ethereum’s double top formation that developed within its $2,196-$3,904 Macro Range.

After breaking down from this range, Ethereum trades within a historical liquidity pool, between the $1,640-$1,930 range, and “effectively has positioned itself for a bearish retest” of the range’s top with its monthly close within this area, which could turn this level into a new resistance.

As the analyst explains, turning this level into resistance has historically seen ETH’s price drop to the current range’s lower zone. “In other words, turning the red level into resistance (red circle) has historically preceded a drop into the support at the bottom of the light blue historical demand area (orange circle),” he detailed.

As such, Ethereum must reclaim the top of this demand area “to challenge a move to the old Macro Range Low of $2,196.” Meanwhile, a rejection from the $1,930 mark, which it has been unable to reclaim over the past week, would see ETH risk a 15% drop to the $1,550 area.

Is A 20% Rally Coming?

Rekt Capital also pointed out that since June 2023, ETH’s Dominance has dropped from 20% to 8%, historically a reverse area for the cryptocurrency. When Ethereum’s Dominance touched the $7.5%-8.25% range, it reversed “to become more market-dominant,” which could signal a reversal for the King of Altcoins.

Several analysts consider that the key levels to watch are the $1,750 support and the $2,100 resistance, as a break above or below these levels will determine ETH’s next significant move.

Analyst Sjuul from AltCryptoGems suggested that Ethereum could eye a 20% rally based on a Power of 3 setup in ETH’s lower timeframe chart. The analyst highlighted that the cryptocurrency had an accumulation phase after dropping below the $2,150 support, hovering within the $1,840 and $2,100 levels since March 10.

After dipping below the $1,840 mark, the cryptocurrency has been in the manipulation phase, the chart shows, which could trigger a push to the $2,150 resistance if ETH breaks out and starts the distribution phase.

As of this writing, Ethereum trades at $1,808, a 2.2% surge in the daily timeframe.

data.title: U.S. Tariffs Spark Hopes for Big-Bang Reforms in India
data.description:
By Tripti Lahiri

In India, some economists are arguing that U.S. pressure may just be what the doctor ordered.

India's growth rates before and after 1991-when a financial crisis sparked a significant opening up of the economy-provided a real-world demonstration of the benefits of lower tariffs, easier rules and more foreign investment. But since about 2010, average tariff rates have risen about five percentage points. In addition, new standards and certification rules on imports, some of them imposed to keep out low-quality Chinese goods, are constraining imports of goods manufacturers need.

These policies have often left manufacturing in the doldrums. Manufacturing's contribution to India's gross domestic product has fallen from around 17% two decades ago to 13% in 2023, according to World Bank data.

Shoumitro Chatterjee, assistant professor of international economics at Johns Hopkins University, says India could grab a bigger piece of global trade if it used the opportunity provided by Trump's shakeup to overcome domestic pressure in order to lower and simplify tariffs.

"I for one really think this is almost like a '91 opportunity for us," said Chatterjee. "These big reforms only happen when there is some kind of external stimulus for reform."

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Tax Swindlers Are Thriving. The Turmoil at the IRS Doesn't Help.
data.description:
By Karen Hube

Taxpayers, be vigilant: Scammers are on the prowl, emboldened by a climate of uncertainty.

Tactics to steal your money have spiked recently and they are getting increasingly sophisticated and difficult to detect.

In January alone this year, some 3,500 fraudulent IRS websites were created to dupe taxpayers into supplying personal information or making payments, "and we've seen almost a 700% increase in traffic on suspicious tax-related domains," says Mark Friedlich, vice president of government affairs at Wolters Kluwer and a longtime advisor to the IRS.

Fake websites are just one tool scammers use. Phone calls, texts, emails and social media posts by criminals impersonating IRS agents or tax preparers aim to get taxpayers to send money or share their Social Security numbers.

With personal information in hand, swindlers steal taxpayers' identities, file tax returns and pocket refunds.

It isn't just taxpayers who are targeted. Criminals trick accountants or employers, get into their systems, and steal sensitive information.

One scam that has spiked this year: Swindlers use AI to replicate the way a company's CEO communicates, then send an email to the firm's human resources department requesting a list of employees and their Form W-2s to prepare for an audit, says John Wilson, senior fellow of threat research at the cybersecurity company Fortra.

"The information is used to file tax returns and claim refunds, hoping to beat the taxpayers to the punch," Wilson says. "When the real taxpayer files later, the IRS says, 'Oh no, you already filed a tax return.'"

While tax season is always a prime time for tax crooks, periods of transition and uncertainty have historically triggered a spike in activity, says Eva Velasquez, CEO of the Identity Theft Resource Center.

As executive orders and ideas for changes roll out of the White House at a rapid pace under the Trump administration, it's easier for bad actors to convince taxpayers they need to act quickly to snag supposed new opportunities.

Velasquez points to the recent idea of Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) stimulus checks as an example of an opportunity for scammers.

In February, Trump floated the idea of DOGE sending $5,000 checks to individuals to share savings from federal cost-cutting measures.

"When people get a phone call or a text message from a supposed preparer saying, 'Hey, do you want to take advantage of the DOGE dividend,' they've caught wind of it already, but they don't know it's not a real opportunity," Velasquez says.

Another scam stems from an IRS announcement in December that it would issue $1,400 payments to taxpayers who were eligible but didn't claim 2021 recovery rebate credits, which were part of pandemic-related economic stimulus.

"Bad actors send text messages pretending to be from the IRS, promising the $1,400," Friedlich says. "They aim to steal personal information and money from individuals."

Your information is also at risk sitting in a tax preparer's computer, Wilson says.

"Something we're seeing a lot of is a scammer sending an email to an accountant, claiming they want to be a new client," Wilson says. "There's usually a good back story, like the tax pro they usually use was impacted by the Palisades fire."

The scammer will ask if they can email their last year's return to get a quote for services. "But what they send is really malware that gives remote control to the preparers' computer and clients' information," Wilson says.

For identity theft victims, resolving cases can be a massive headache: Recouping refunds takes an average of 22 months, according to the Taxpayer Advocate Service.

When it suspects fraud, the IRS holds returns and freezes refunds, but half are falsely identified as fraudulent, according to the Taxpayer Advocate. In 2024, four weeks into the tax season, 1.9 million returns claiming refunds of $16.5 billion were held pending identity verification, according to the Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration.

Taxpayers often must verify identities at IRS walk-in offices, but many are closing under DOGE cuts. Of 360 taxpayer assistance offices, 113 are getting shuttered.

"How will people reach IRS offices? It's going to be a nightmare," Friedlich says.

Meanwhile, taxpayer defenses could be eroding at the IRS.

The IRS started improving fraud detection technology with funds provided under the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act. But modernization efforts were paused in March, and last week the DOGE ax fell on 50 IRS information technology leaders working on artificial-intelligence-enabled fraud detection.

While 7,000 IRS workers fired in February can now take their jobs back under a federal court ruling, the 50 fired technology workers aren't part of the rehiring order, according to a source close to the matter.

Meanwhile, the IRS's annual budget for modernization has been zeroed out, and IRA funds allocated for technology upgrades are likely to be depleted by next year, says Janet Holtzblatt, a senior fellow at the Urban-Brookings Tax Policy Center. "Modernization was looking grim even before the pause was announced," she says.

The White House, asked about the pause in modernization and closure of IRS offices, said: "President Trump has made it clear that he is committed to making the federal government more efficient without compromising mission-critical operations. There will be no disruptions to service."

How can you protect yourself? Assume any text, phone call or email that purports to be from the IRS is a scam. The IRS always initiates communication through the U.S. Postal Service.

If someone contacts you claiming to be a tax preparer, check credentials. You can use the IRS's directory of federal tax return preparers on the agency's website ( https://irs.treasury.gov/rpo/rpo.jsf)

Finally, request a personal identification number (PIN) from the IRS. Your return will then only be accepted by the IRS with the PIN. "You have to get a PIN reissued each year," Wilson says. "But I assure you, you'd much rather do that than have the IRS say you already filed your tax return, when you actually haven't."

Philippines alarmed over China arrest of alleged Filipino spies
data.description:
The Philippines expressed alarm on Saturday over the arrest of three Filipinos in China on suspicion of espionage, saying they were ordinary citizens and the arrests could be retaliation for Manila's crackdown against alleged Chinese spies.

Chinese authorities arrested the Filipinos and accused them of working for the Philippine intelligence agency to gather classified information on its military, the state-run China Daily reported earlier this week, citing state security officials. It said the three had confessed to the crime.

The Philippines' National Security Council disputed Beijing's accusations, saying the three were former recipients of a government scholarship programme created under an agreement between the southern Chinese province of Hainan and the western Philippine province of Palawan.

"They are ordinary Filipino citizens with no military training who merely went to China at the invitation of the Chinese government to study," National Security Council spokesperson Jonathan Malaya said in a statement.

"They are law-abiding citizens with no criminal records and were vetted and screened by the Chinese government prior to their arrival there," he added.

The Chinese embassy in Manila did not immediately respond to a request for comment outside office hours.

Hainan and Palawan both face the South China Sea, a strategic waterway where the two countries have staked out overlapping claims and have clashed frequently over the past two years.

"The arrests can be seen as a retaliation for the series of legitimate arrests of Chinese agents and accomplices by Philippine law enforcement," Malaya said.

Philippine authorities have arrested at least a dozen Chinese nationals in the last three months on suspicion of espionage, accusing them of illegally obtaining sensitive information on military camps and critical infrastructure that could undermine Manila's national security and defence.

China has expansive territorial claims in the South China Sea that overlap with the exclusive economic zones of Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines and Vietnam. In 2016, an international arbitral tribunal ruled China's claims have no basis under international law, although Beijing does not recognise that ruling.

data.title: Italy says counter-tariffs would be wrong response to U.S. tariffs
data.description:
Italian Economy Minister Giancarlo Giorgetti warned on Saturday against the imposition of retaliatory tariffs on the United States in response to U.S President Donald Trump's announcement of sweeping tariffs on trade partners.

Speaking at a business forum near Milan, Giorgetti said Italy was aiming for a "de-escalation" with the U.S.

"We should avoid launching a policy of counter-tariffs that could be damaging for everyone and especially for us," Giorgetti said.

Under Trump's plans Italy, which has a large trade surplus with the United States, will be subject to a general tariff of 20% along with other European Union countries.

data.title: Ukrainian drones strike industrial facility in Russia's Mordovia, governor says
data.description:
An industrial facility in Russia's Volga river region of Mordovia was hit by a drone strike early on Saturday, the region's governor said in a statement on Telegram.

Governor Artyom Zdunov said that emergency services were at the scene of the strike on the facility, which he did not name, and that there had been no casualties.

Baza, a Telegram channel close to Russian law enforcement, said the target had been an optical fibre factory in the city of Saransk, 820km (510 miles) from the Ukrainian border.

It published a video of a blast in the night sky that it said showed the strike.

data.title: Nissan considers transferring some domestic vehicle production to US, Nikkei says
data.description:
Nissan Motor is considering shifting some domestic production of U.S.-bound vehicles to the U.S. as early as this summer to avoid export to the country, the Nikkei reported on Saturday.

The Japanese automaker plans to reduce production at its Fukuoka factory and shift some manufacturing of its Rogue SUV to the United States to mitigate the impact of additional tariffs imposed by President Donald Trump's administration, the report said, without citing the source of its information.

Nissan's Rogue SUV, a key model in the U.S. market, is currently produced both in Fukuoka and the United States, the report said.
